one_line_takeaway: A label-free denoising-autoencoder purifier, trained with diversified malware perturbations, protective benign-noise injection, and a reconstruction-plus-prediction dual loss, is bolted in front of an unchanged Android malware detector and holds robust accuracy above ~90% across 37+ evasion attacks (black/grey/white-box and structural), except against Mimicry attacks.
key_finding: MalPurifier robustly defends the stated 37 perturbation-based evasion attacks with robust accuracy consistently above 90.91%, outperforming all baselines especially against unseen and adaptive attacks. On clean data (Table 1) it costs only a small accuracy drop (97.00% Drebin, 98.57% Androzoo vs 98.72%/99.23% for plain DNN) while keeping FPR low (2.55%/2.30%), and it trains far faster than the strongest prior defense PAD-SMA (750s/696s vs 21,627s/29,837s). Under white-box attacks it achieves the highest accuracy on all 17 attacks on Drebin (>=90.91%) and 15 on Androzoo (>=97.44%), whereas baselines including PAD-SMA collapse on unseen attacks (e.g. PGD-l1, MaxMA). Against advanced structural attacks (RQ7) it reaches up to 92.38% (Drebin) / 94.38% (Androzoo), well above FD-VAE and PAD-SMA. The DAE also transfers as a plug-in to SVM/FCN/LSTM/RNN, raising RNN accuracy under Pointwise from 0% to 99.72%/99.82%.
core_contribution: MalPurifier is an adversarial-purification defense for ML-based Android malware detectors. Rather than hardening the classifier via adversarial training, it inserts a lightweight, model-agnostic, plug-and-play Denoising AutoEncoder (DAE) as a pre-processing step that projects (possibly perturbed) feature vectors back toward the clean malware/benign manifold before the unchanged detector classifies them. Its three claimed innovations are: (1) a diversified adversarial-perturbation mechanism (Algorithm 1) that generates training malware with progressively increasing perturbation depth from zero to worst-case, to generalize to unseen attacks; (2) a protective-noise-injection strategy (Algorithm 2) that randomly flips a small fraction of benign features during training so the purifier learns to preserve legitimate apps and avoid over-purification / false positives, motivated by the asymmetric threat model in which adversaries only perturb malware; and (3) a DAE trained with a dual-objective loss combining reconstruction loss (MSE between purified and original feature vector) and a prediction loss that aligns the purified sample with the original in the detector's internal (nth-layer) feature space (overall loss alpha*Lrec + beta*Lpre, with alpha=beta=0.5). The DAE is trained independently of class labels and does not require retraining the detector.
threat_model: Test-time evasion attacks against Android malware detectors: an adversary perturbs a malware sample z->z' (feature space x->x') to be misclassified as benign while preserving malicious functionality, formalized as a box constraint x' in [u_lo, u_hi] with perturbations restricted to non-functional manifest/dexcode additions (e.g. unused permissions, unreachable API-call insertions). Three knowledge levels are considered: black-box (attacker knows neither detector f nor purifier g; e.g. obfuscation attacks), grey-box / oblivious (attacker knows the baseline DNN classifier f but not the purifier g), and white-box / adaptive (attacker has full knowledge of both f and g, including 'orthogonal' attacks that split perturbation budget across detector and purifier). The paper evaluates against a stated 37 perturbation-based evasion attacks plus additional structural/problem-space attacks.
limitations: The authors explicitly report that MalPurifier cannot effectively resist the Mimicry attack (accuracy <=60%): because the DAE purifies malware that closely resembles benign apps toward the benign manifold, such samples get misclassified as benign, and the weakness worsens as the mimicry target is more benign-like. They also concede that a fully adaptive white-box adversary with complete knowledge of both model and purifier could in principle craft attacks that circumvent purification, since the detector itself is never re-trained or modified. Other reviewer-noted caveats: results are on a single classifier architecture (small DNN) with static feature spaces; the '37 attacks >90.91%' headline aggregates many closely related PGD/ensemble variants; the Drebin dataset predates 2013 (temporal drift), and improvement is measured against reimplemented baselines rather than an external leaderboard.

datasets_used: Drebin (5,560 malicious samples; 47,770 benign APKs downloaded from markets e.g. Google Play/AppChina/Anzhi; top 10,000 static binary features from manifest+dexcode; collected pre-2013)
AndroZoo (170,851 APKs from 2021; 10,987 benign + 10,998 malicious sampled; VirusTotal-labeled, malicious if >=5 scanners flag, benign if 0 flags)
MaMaDroid-style graph features (call-graph package-transition Markov chain, PCA to 10 components) used only in RQ7
quantitative_results: Clean-data accuracy: MalPurifier 97.00% (Drebin) / 98.57% (Androzoo) vs plain DNN 98.72% / 99.23%
Black-box obfuscation (RQ2): 100% accuracy on all 8 attacks on Drebin; >=95% on 7 of 8 attacks on Androzoo
Grey-box (RQ3): robust accuracy >=90.91% (Drebin) and >=99.24% (Androzoo) across gradient/gradient-free/ensemble attacks; e.g. StepwiseMA MalPurifier 96.66% (Drebin) vs KDE 0.649%
White-box (RQ4, Table 3): highest accuracy on all 17 attacks (Drebin, >=90.91%) and 15 attacks (Androzoo, >=97.44%); e.g. PGD-l1 Drebin 99.72% vs PAD-SMA 77.83%, DNN 0%

Structural attacks (RQ4/Table 4): MalPurifier Drebin RA 79.74 / MAB 90.88 / AdvDroidZero 87.85 / EvadeDroid 92.38 / HRAT 92.08; Androzoo up to 94.38 (EvadeDroid); next-best PAD-SMA/FD-VAE trail by 17.76-37.3 points
Purifier comparison (RQ6): DAE >=95.08% (Drebin) and >=99.24% (Androzoo) across attacks; VAE/PCA/GAN/DM drop to as low as 0% under some attacks
Transferability (RQ6): DAE plug-in raises accuracy by >=39.61% (SVM), >=87.71% (FCN), >=30.47% (LSTM), >=7.97% (RNN); RNN under Pointwise 0% -> 99.72% (Drebin) / 99.82% (Androzoo)
Interpretability (RQ5): residual perturbation-vs-purification gap <=12.2% (Drebin, Hardware under MaxMA) and <=2.4% (Androzoo)
baselines_compared: KDE (kernel-density-estimate secondary detector on final layer)
AT-rFGSMk (adversarial training with randomized-rounding FGSMk / PGD-linf)
PAD-SMA (principled adversarial detection with ICNN + Stepwise Mixture of Attacks)
Purification alternatives (RQ6): VAE, PCA, GAN, Diffusion Model
Transfer target detectors (RQ6): SVM, FCN, LSTM, RNN
